aliases: Chinese Herbal Pork Rib Noodle Soup

english: Chinese Herbal Pork Rib Noodle Soup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for herbal spare rib noodle soup. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Noodles, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for herbal pork rib noodle soup in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific dish. SNOMED CT coverage of prepared Asian noodle dishes is limited.

main_ingredients: Chinese medicinal herbs (dang gui, goji berries, red dates, dried longan, wolfberries)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of herbal spare rib noodle is approximately 500 g, comprising a bowl of noodles in herbal pork broth with two to three pieces of braised spare ribs.

nutrient_source.title: Pork rib noodle soup, estimated from component analysis: cooked wheat noodles (USDA FoodData Central #20121), pork spare ribs braised (USDA FoodData Central #10124), and Chinese herbal broth estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for pork bone soup
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central; FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for herbal spare rib noodle exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from weighted component analysis: approximately 45% cooked wheat noodles, 30% braised pork spare rib meat and bone, 25% herbal broth. USDA SR Legacy data used for noodles and pork; FAO/INFOODS SEA used for broth estimation. Per-100g values reflect the composite bowl.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat noodles and mixed pork noodle soup dishes; analogous estimate based on cooked wheat noodles GI (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, 2008, International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values, Diabetes Care)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for herbal spare rib noodle. GI estimated at 47 based on cooked wheat noodles (GI ~47, University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables). The protein and fat content from pork ribs and herbal broth further attenuate the glycemic response relative to plain noodles. GL per serving calculated as (47 × 62.5 g available carbohydrate per 500 g serving) / 100 = 29.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from pork bone broth and soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of herbal spare rib noodle due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (2100 mg per 500 g serving), which represents a substantial portion of the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target recommended for hypertensive patients; requesting reduced-sodium broth or consuming less soup liquid is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles, partially offset by protein and fat from pork ribs
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should be aware that a standard 500 g serving carries a glycemic load of approximately 29, which is in the high GL range; portion control is recommended, and substituting with lower-GI noodles such as glass noodles or reducing noodle quantity while increasing vegetable content can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork spare ribs and moderate sodium from broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume herbal spare rib noodle in moderation given its saturated fat content (approximately 1.5 g per 100 g) and high sodium load; the Chinese medicinal herbs including dang gui and goji berries may offer antioxidant benefits, but these do not offset the cardiovascular risk from frequent high-sodium, moderate-saturated-fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Herbal Spare Rib Noodle (药膳排骨面, Yào Shàn Pái Gǔ Miàn) is a Chinese noodle soup dish popular in Singapore and among Chinese communities in Southeast Asia. It consists of wheat or egg noodles served in a rich, aromatic broth made by slow-simmering pork spare ribs with traditional Chinese medicinal herbs such as dang gui (angelica root), goji berries (wolfberries), red dates (hong zao), dried longan, and sometimes astragalus root. The dish is seasoned with soy sauce, garlic, and ginger. The broth is deeply savoury with a subtle herbal sweetness and a warm, slightly earthy aroma characteristic of Chinese medicinal cooking. The spare ribs are braised until tender and fall off the bone. The noodles absorb the herbal broth, creating a satisfying, warming bowl. This dish is considered nourishing in traditional Chinese medicine, believed to tonify blood, warm the body, and support qi. Nutritionally, it provides moderate protein from pork, carbohydrates from noodles, and a range of micronutrients including iron, zinc, and B vitamins. The sodium content is high due to the broth and soy sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 47 (low) based on wheat noodle analogues, but the glycemic load per serving is moderate to high at approximately 29. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans and is not halal due to pork content. Served as a main meal at lunch or dinner, it is a comfort food with cultural significance in Chinese culinary tradition.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for herbal spare rib noodle was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional food composition database. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked wheat noodles and braised pork spare ribs, supplemented by FAO/INFOODS SEA estimates for herbal broth. GI is estimated by analogy from cooked wheat noodles per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated; no single approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. Values should be validated against laboratory analysis or an HPB Singapore Foodo entry if one becomes available. 2. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported in the component sources used for this estimation. 3. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for pork spare ribs and noodles at the composite level. 4. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source data for this dish type. 5. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component sources. 6. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component sources. 7. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for the pork and noodle components — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 8. The dish contains pork and is therefore not halal or kosher. 9. Herbal ingredients (dang gui, goji berries, red dates) may interact with anticoagulant medications; clinicians should note this for patients on warfarin. 10. GI category is low (GI = 47, which is ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules).
